front: Play: the protagonist plays chess with himself, disguises himself with a papier-mache nose to mimic the inaugurator Maxy Falk, and learns to speak in bureaucratic cliches at the Liquidation Office
Play: the protagonist proposes swapping Small Dance Floor C and Large Dance Floor A
Play: the protagonist's parents send his brother Peter into a pantry for being a 'bourgeois intellectual'
Play: the Pludek family uses nonsensical proverbs like 'lentils are lentils and oats are oats' and 'Not even a hag would go in the woods without a clamp!'
Play: the protagonist gains control of the Liquidation Office and pits it against the Inauguration Service
Play: Josef Gross cannot read a memo written in the fictional bureaucratic language Ptydepe; the language Chorukor replaces Ptydepe at the end
Play: secretary Maria insists she cannot translate the fictional language without permission; the protagonist urges her not to lose hope at the end
Play: the author applied Shannon entropy (learned from his computer scientist brother) to design the fictional language in this play
Play: the fictional language's shortest word is 'gh' (meaning 'whatever'); its longest word (319 letters) means 'wombat'
Play: the protagonist is tricked into signing a document by a man who spreads a rumor that he takes a rubber stamp home and lets his children play with it
Author: this essay opens 'A specter is haunting Eastern Europe' (echoing Marx) and describes a greengrocer who displays 'Workers of the world, unite!' in his window out of fear
Author: argues in an essay that 'I am afraid and therefore unquestioningly obedient' would be more accurate for a greengrocer's sign; discusses 'post-totalitarian' systems and 'small-scale work'
Play: the neurotic Leopold Nettles is a dissident driven to distraction by constant state surveillance
Play: the student Marguerite boasts about reading Ontology of the Human Self, Phenomenology of Responsibility, and Love and Nothingness by a professor in a dressing gown
Author: created the autobiographical dissident Ferdinand Vanek, forced to work at a brewery and offered the task of writing reports for the secret police, in a quartet of plays
Play: draws from Goethe's Faust; the wizard Fistula smells like cheese and is revealed as an agent of the Director at a 'witches sabbath party'
Author: prison letters to his wife contain an early formulation of 'existential revolution'; cites Saul Bellow's line 'To God, he jotted several lines' from Herzog
Author: founding signatory of Charter 77; helped draft it after the arrest of the Plastic People of the Universe
Author: claimed to be inspired to run for president by the Velvet Underground / Lou Reed
Author: wrote experimental concrete poems called 'anticodes'
